does: This paper presents Spread Preference Annotation (SPA), a framework for aligning large language models using minimal human-annotated preference data. The core method iteratively generates model responses and learns from self-annotated preferences derived directly from the model's logits, extracting the LLM's inherent preference signals without external reward models. The approach progressively improves alignment by leveraging human priors from a small seed dataset and employing a noise-aware preference learning algorithm to handle quality issues in self-generated annotations. The authors demonstrate significant efficiency gains compared to methods relying on external reward models or implicit in-context learning approaches.
connects: This work builds on the line of research addressing the high annotation cost of preference-based LLM alignment, extending beyond RLHF and recent efficient alternatives like online preference learning. It responds to limitations of external reward model approaches by proposing direct preference extraction from model logits, relating to self-training and bootstrapping methods in machine learning. The noise-aware learning component connects to work on handling noisy labels in preference learning. The iterative improvement strategy draws on active learning and curriculum learning principles, while the focus on minimal supervision links to few-shot and semi-supervised learning traditions in NLP.
enables: This approach opens directions for scalable LLM alignment with dramatically reduced human annotation requirements, potentially democratizing preference-based fine-tuning across resource-constrained settings. The direct logit-based preference extraction method could inspire new ways to interpret and leverage model internal signals for self-improvement. The noise-aware learning framework could be adapted to other self-training scenarios with unreliable generated data. Future work might explore combining this approach with active learning to optimally select which samples humans should annotate, or extending it to multi-dimensional preferences and long-horizon tasks where preference signals are more ambiguous.
